section: Chapter 4: Linear Equations in Two Variables

concepts.lesson: A linear equation in two variables is an equation that can be written in the form ax + by + c = 0, where a, b, and c are real numbers and a and b are not both zero. The variables x and y represent unknown quantities. Because the equation contains two variables, a single value cannot determine the solution. Instead, a pair of values (x, y) that satisfies the equation forms a solution. Linear equations of this form represent relationships between two quantities and appear frequently in real-life situations such as cost calculations, distance relationships, and scoring problems.
concepts.real_example: x + y = 176 representing runs scored by two players

concepts.lesson: Many equations involving two variables may appear in different forms such as x + y = 5 or x − 3y = 4. However, they can always be rewritten into the standard form ax + by + c = 0. Converting equations into this form helps identify the coefficients of the variables clearly. It also allows easier comparison between different equations and simplifies many algebraic procedures. Understanding this form helps students analyse the structure of linear equations and recognise them quickly.
key_points: Standard form is ax + by + c = 0

concepts.concept_name: Solution of Linear Equation in Two Variables
concepts.lesson: A solution of a linear equation in two variables is an ordered pair (x, y) that satisfies the equation when substituted into it. Because there are two variables, infinitely many ordered pairs may satisfy the equation. Each valid pair produces equality when substituted into the equation. These solutions represent points on a coordinate plane and collectively form a straight line. Understanding this concept helps students see equations not just as algebraic expressions but as geometric relationships.
key_points: Solution is an ordered pair (x, y)

concepts.lesson: Unlike linear equations in one variable, which usually have a single solution, linear equations in two variables have infinitely many solutions. This happens because there are countless combinations of x and y that can satisfy the same equation. For example, by choosing different values for x and calculating the corresponding value of y, we obtain many solution pairs. This property shows that a linear equation in two variables represents a continuous relationship between two quantities.

Solutions of equations in two variables are written as ordered pairs in the form (x, y). The first value represents the value of x and the second value represents the value of y. The order is important because reversing the values changes the meaning of the pair. Ordered pairs help represent algebraic solutions visually when plotted on a coordinate plane. This concept forms the bridge between algebra and coordinate geometry.

To check whether a pair of numbers is a solution of a linear equation, substitute the values of x and y into the equation. If both sides become equal after substitution, the pair is a valid solution. If the equality does not hold, the pair is not a solution. This process ensures accuracy when identifying valid ordered pairs and helps students verify their answers logically.
concepts.real_example: For equation 2x + 3y = 12, (1,4) is not a solution because it gives 14

concepts.lesson: Every solution of a linear equation in two variables corresponds to a point on a coordinate plane. When several solutions are plotted, they lie on a straight line. This line represents the graph of the equation. The graphical representation helps visualise the relationship between the variables and provides a geometric interpretation of algebraic equations. Graphs make it easier to understand trends and relationships between quantities.
concepts.real_example: Plotting solutions of x + y = 6 forms a straight line

concepts.lesson: A linear equation and its graph represent the same mathematical relationship in two different forms. Algebraically, the equation describes the relationship between variables, while geometrically the graph shows this relationship as a straight line. Every point on the line satisfies the equation, and every solution of the equation lies somewhere on the line. This concept is fundamental in coordinate geometry and helps link algebraic reasoning with visual understanding.
concepts.real_example: All points on the line x + y = 5 satisfy the equation
key_points: Every point on line is a solution
Important link between algebra and geometry
